_description: THE WARM HANDOFF — a social engineering campaign that successfully planted a new vendor contact into the trusted relationship 6 months ago, then went dormant. The current invoice passes every BEC check: the email comes from sarah.chen@archer-cyber.com, a known domain, with clean headers, from a signatory in sender_history with 9 emails of established history. Sarah Chen is in vendor_record.known_contacts. The amount is within range. The account is unchanged. Nothing about the current invoice is suspicious. The attack already happened — 6 months ago. The email_chain includes the introduction email that started everything: sent March 15, 2025 from David Park's address (d.park@archer-cyber.com), introducing Sarah Chen as the new billing coordinator. Professional, plausible, unremarkable. Solo Turn 1 evaluates the current invoice: domain clean, account unchanged, amount in range, known signatory with 6 months of history, Sarah Chen in vendor_record.known_contacts. ALLOW. Solo Turn 2 (same model, anchored to Turn 1's clean checklist) confirms. ALLOW. Neither turn reads the introduction email's raw headers. The From header of the introduction email displays archer-cyber.com — correct. But the Return-Path and Reply-To in the raw_headers show d.park@archer-cybersec.com — a lookalike domain. A different domain entirely. The introduction email that inserted Sarah Chen into the vendor relationship, into sender_history, into known_contacts — was spoofed. Every trust signal associated with Sarah Chen was manufactured from that one email. The current email from sarah.chen@archer-cyber.com is clean because the attacker registered archer-cyber.com as their operational domain after the initial compromise via archer-cybersec.com. Six months of history, a contact record, a clean sender profile — all built on a spoofed introduction. Holo Turn 2 (different model, adversarial, no checklist anchoring) reads the full email_chain — all three entries, including the oldest. Introduction email from March 15, 2025: From header shows archer-cyber.com. Return-Path: d.park@archer-cybersec.com. Reply-To: d.park@archer-cybersec.com. The email that introduced Sarah Chen came from a different domain than the one on file. Sarah Chen's entire presence in this vendor relationship stems from that one email. Every trust signal since then is downstream of that compromise. ESCALATE.

email_chain.subject: Introducing our new billing coordinator — Sarah Chen
email_chain.body:
Hi Daniel,

Just a quick note — we've brought on Sarah Chen to handle billing coordination going forward. She'll be your main point of contact for invoices and payment questions. I'll still be around for contract and relationship matters.

Sarah's email: sarah.chen@archer-cyber.com

Please update your records accordingly. Thanks as always for the partnership.

Best,
David Park
Senior Account Manager
Archer Cyber Solutions LLC

context.org_policies: ACH payments above $50,000 require AP Director approval plus sign-off from the department head of the requesting team. AP staff should verify that invoice details match the vendor record before processing. Any questions about a vendor relationship should be directed to the vendor's account manager on file.
